# Extra ZAPs generated by COBOL after packed arithmetic function

_42 messages · 11 participants · 2014-02 → 2014-04_

---

### Extra ZAPs generated by COBOL after packed arithmetic function

- **From:** "steveuk529" <ua-author-14501714@usenetarchives.gap>
- **Date:** 2014-02-04T18:10:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209bcc8174aa@googlegroups.com>`

```
This problem/issue has been discussed at length in other threads. ZAP is generated to correct the sign if the result is -0 after overflow. We have requested that IBM offer a compiler option to eliminate this ZAP since it is causing performance issues in a large, packed-decimal arithmetic intensive job.

I understand that these RFEs (Request for Evaluations) are voted on by the RFE community so if you are an IBM developerWorks member, agree that this performance enhancement would make sense in your environment then feel free to vote. RFE ID 44362.
```

#### ↳ Re: Extra ZAPs generated by COBOL after packed arithmetic function

- **From:** "doug_at_milmac_dot_com" <ua-author-176133@usenetarchives.gap>
- **Date:** 2014-02-04T19:49:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **In-Reply-To:** `<0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209bcc8174aa@googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209bcc8174aa@googlegroups.com>`

```
ste··9@gm··l.com wrote in
news:0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209··a@goo··s.com:

› This problem/issue has been discussed at length in other
› threads.  ZAP is generated to correct the sign if the result is
› -0 after overflow.  We have requested that IBM offer a compiler
› option to eliminate this ZAP since it is causing performance
› issues in a large, packed-decimal arithmetic intensive job. 

I find it hard to imagine that the performance issues are due to the extra ZAP; it's much more
likely that the performance problems result from the actual computations.

If you really are generating overflows that result in -0 often enough for the unneeded ZAP to be
causing a measurable performance impact -- then you need to fix the problems either in your
code or in your data that are causing the overflows.
```

In article <5cf282d9-ca8e-46c6-958a-8c6··7@goo··s.com>,
wrote:
› On Wednesday, February 5, 2014 10:10:19 AM UTC+11, ste··.@gm··l.com wrote:
›› This problem/issue has been discussed at length in other threads.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
› a suitable test(s) prior to doing the arithmetic?

Here's a series of actions that the faint-of-heart might find shocking...
but fortune favors the bold!

1) Find a set of statements which cause the situation in question, such as
COMPUTE WEEKLY-GROSS-PAY = ((HOURLY-RATE * FLAT-HOURS) + (OT-RATE *
OT-HOURS)).

2) Post this set of statements, along with the data definitions of all
fields involved.

3) Post the compiler version and options used to compile.

4) Post the PMAP... errrrr, LIST section showing the ASM generated by the
code.

5) Mention the approximate transaction volume and a rough percentage of
input records/rows that cause the difficulties.

6) Ask whatever Fellow Professionals you can rouse from their torpor what
they might think of the situation.

DD
```

The point of the OP's message seems to have been lost.

He isn't asking for speculation about how ZAP may affect performance (he is
already persuaded that it does, under certain circumstances); rather, he
wants people to support his RFE.

If you are someone who qualifies to vote on RFEs, and you think he has a
point, then you may wish to vote in support of his suggestion.

If you are sceptical, then you may vote against it.

I'm no longer a member of any mainframe User Group so I don't have a dog in
this fight and have no right to vote, but, if I had such enfranchisement, I
think I would want to see much more convincing evidence than has been
provided so far...

Pete.
"I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."

Doug Miller wrote:
› "Pete Dashwood"  wrote in
› news:blisfgFq8p8U1 @mid.individual.net:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
› In my opinion, the OP never had a valid point to begin with.

And you reached that opinion after examining all of his source code, his
processing and the timings of his jobs?

I'm also, based on my own mainframe experience which covered around 20
years, sceptical of his claim. I've never seen ZAP or CVD as great
timewasting instructions, although it is true that the decimal instruction
set was not part of Gene Amdahl's original design for System 360 and was
implemented through firmware as an additional extra which had to be
purchased for commercial data processing. But those days are long ago and
far away; modern systems do not go stepping through Read Only Storage in
order to execute the decimal instruction set. Nevertheless, just because my
experience tells me this should not be a problem, here's someone who says it
IS a problem.

He is SO persuaded that it is, that his company is requesting IBM to add a
compiler option to deal with it.

To immediately decide he has no point is not an action I would condone. My
own experience over 48 years in this industry has been that sometimes
unexpected results can happen. And sometimes even things you think work a
certain way can misbehave... Everyone deserves the benefit of the doubt
until the problem has been investigated. Speculation is fine for the coffee
lounge but the shop floor requires diagnostics, traces, analysis and
investigation.

Given the infinite variety of ways in which code can be coded, perhaps there
IS some combination of circumstances where ZAP becomes problematic.

I've never seen it but that doesn't mean it can't happen.

› 
›› He isn't asking for speculation about how ZAP may affect performance
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
› proposition.

Yes, I would agree it is unlikely (and I said so in the final sentence
quoted below), but that makes it even more interesting if he proves to be
right.
›› 
›› If you are someone who qualifies to vote on RFEs, and you think he
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
›› 

The OP is asking for support with an RFE.

Maybe if he was asked to provide actual evidence (as I believe Doc
suggested, and I would also endorse that) he would have more chance of
getting that support.

However, there may be people who will support the RFE just because they like
seeing IBM do work and whether they ever use the new compiler option or not,
they may feel that the more compiler options there are, the better. :-)

(Who would dare read the minds of an IBM User Group... ?)

So, the OP has lost nothing by taking a shot and posting here...

Pete.
"I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209bcc8174aa@googlegroups.com> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p8@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p9@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p10@usenetarchives.gap>`

```
On Sat, 8 Feb 2014 12:01:55 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
wrote:

› Doug Miller wrote:
›› "Pete Dashwood"  wrote in
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
› IS a problem.


While its somewhat hard to know what Amdahl originally had in mind,
the S/360 was intended, from the very start, to support both
commercial and scientific, consolidating IBM's two main lines of
machines. At the time that would have made decimal support mandatory,
although not necessarily in the form implemented in S/360.

While most of the S/360s were microcoded, most (all?) had hardware
dedicated* to decimal support, and in at least some cases, installing
the commercial (aka decimal) feature required the installation of
additional hardware, as did the scientific (FP) feature. In both
cases new microcode was required as well.


*And that obviously would have varied by machine - on the -30 the
support would likely have been very simple, requiring many microcycles
to execute the decimal instructions.
```

› Doug Miller wrote:

…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
› processing and the timings of his jobs?

No, I reached that opinion on the basis of my understanding of what does, and what does
not, cause performance problems.
› 
› I'm also, based on my own mainframe experience which covered around 20 
› years, sceptical of his claim. 
 
› "Skeptical" is IMHO an understatement...
 
› I've never seen ZAP or CVD as great 
› timewasting instructions, although it is true that the decimal instruction 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
› IS a problem.
 
› And my experience tells me that he's far more likely to be wrong than right.
› 
› He is SO persuaded that it is, that his company is requesting IBM to add a 
› compiler option to deal with it.

I'm confident that IBM has much more evidence that the extra ZAPs don't cause
performance issues, than he's ever going to be able to produce that they do.
›
› To immediately decide he has no point is not an action I would condone. My
› own experience over 48 years in this industry has been that sometimes
› unexpected results can happen.

Has not that experience also taught you that "unexpected results" are *always* due to
mistaken expectations on the part of human observer(s)?

› And sometimes even things you think work a
› certain way can misbehave...

Computers do not "misbehave". They behave as they have been instructed to behave.
When behavior does not conform to expectations, that is the result of incorrect expectations,
or incorrect instructions, not misbehavior.

› Everyone deserves the benefit of the doubt
› until the problem has been investigated.

I disagree. Facially absurd claims manifestly do *not* deserve the benefit of the doubt.

And the OP's claim is facially absurd.

› Speculation is fine for the coffee
› lounge but the shop floor requires diagnostics, traces, analysis and
› investigation.

So ask the OP to produce those diagnostics, traces, analyses, and investigations. He's the
one that claims it's a problem, let him produce the evidence to support that claim.
›
› Given the infinite variety of ways in which code can be coded, perhaps there
› IS some combination of circumstances where ZAP becomes problematic.
›
› I've never seen it but that doesn't mean it can't happen.

There's really not much functional difference between "vanishingly unlikely" and "can't
happen." Consider that the ZAP to which the OP objects is necessarily the last in a chain of
arithmetic instructions, making it only one of several, perhaps one of many. Which is more
likely, that the ZAP -- a very fast instruction -- is the problem, or that the OP is wrong about
where the problem is?

››› He isn't asking for speculation about how ZAP may affect performance
››› (he is already persuaded that it does, under certain circumstances);
 
››› And he's almost certain to be mistaken.
 
››› rather, he wants people to support his RFE.
›› 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
› right.

So far, he hasn't offered any evidence.
```

On 2/9/2014 8:07 PM, Clark F Morris wrote:
› On Mon, 10 Feb 2014 11:47:28 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
›  wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
› (snip)

I read bit.listserv.ibm-main, but I am not registered to post there.

I could be wrong, but my understanding was the sign fix-up ZAP
instruction was needed for NUMPROC(PFD). In other words if the packed
decimal signs were normalized, the generated COBOL Program could use a
faster CLC (compare logic character) instruction instead of a slower CP
(Compare Packed-decimal) instruction.

If that is true, then it is possible removing the sign fix-up ZAP
instruction might actually make packed decimal performance worse.

Kind regards,


http://www.arnoldtrembley.com/
```

› Gene Amdahl's original design for System 360

The S/360 was designed for scientific and commercial users alike.
I very much doubt your claim that the original design did not include
decimal instructions. IBM's commercial users were accustomed to having
decimal instructions. They would be unlikely to be persuaded to upgrade
to S/360 when (1) they could not run their existing object programs
on S/360 under emulation (with decimal instructions), and
(2) run new commercial programs without a decimal instruction set.

The lower models had a basic instruction set (the integer instructions),
to which could be added a choice of decimal and floating-point sets.

› and was implemented through firmware
 
› actually, microcode, as were most of the instructions, depending on the model.
 
› as an additional extra which had to be purchased for commercial data processing.
 
› But those days are long ago and far away; modern systems do not go
› stepping through Read Only Storage in order to execute the decimal
› instruction set.

› Nevertheless, just because my experience tells me this should not be a
› problem, here's someone who says it IS a problem.

Just because he says it is a problem doen't MAKE it a problem.
He THINKS that there is a problem, but has not investigated it
adequately to ascertain the root cause.

› He is SO persuaded that it is, that his company is requesting IBM
› to add a compiler option to deal with it.

Have you/he considered what will happen with -0 results being
propagated to subsequent computations?
Why do you think that IBM has this instruction (ZAP) there?

› To immediately decide he has no point is not an action I would condone.

A basic rule from early days in computer programming is to believe nothing
of what your're told, and only half of what you see.

- **From:** "robertwessel2" <ua-author-4329810@usenetarchives.gap>
- **Date:** 2014-02-10T15:38:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gap-4e61c2a6d9-p19@usenetarchives.gap>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<gap-4e61c2a6d9-p17@usenetarchives.gap>`
- **References:** `<0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209bcc8174aa@googlegroups.com> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p8@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p9@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p10@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p16@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p17@usenetarchives.gap>`

```
On Tue, 11 Feb 2014 01:56:44 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
wrote:

› rob··s@gm··l.com wrote:
›› On Saturday, February 8, 2014 10:01:55 AM UTC+11, Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
› optional extra.


The S/360 architectural definition had the standard instructions set
(basically the fixed point binary instructions) as a base, and had the
decimal and FP instructions as options (along with a number of other
things, for example memory protection - aka storage keys - was a
separate feature, as was the direct control feature). Depending on
the model, those were actual options, or not. For example, on the
360/30 they were options, take a look at pages 9-11 of:

http://bitsavers.trailing-edge.com/pdf/ibm/360/funcChar/GA24-3231-7_360-30_funcChar.pdf

The higher end models (the 360/50 for example) mostly implemented the
"universal" instruction set (the standard set, plus decimal, FP and
storage protection) as the base for the model.



›› IBM's commercial users were accustomed to
›› having decimal instructions.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
› programmed it and I might be confusing it with the NCR 315.)


It was six bits, plus a word mark bit, plus a (hidden, but constantly
talked about in the documentation) parity bit. So physically storage
was eight bits wide, but only seven were visible. Yes, one decimal
digit per storage position (or a six bit character for non-numeric
fields). Negative signs were handled with an overpunch in the zone
bits of the least significant digit (much like a over punch on a
display numeric field on S/360).
```

Robert Wessel wrote:
› On Tue, 11 Feb 2014 01:56:44 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
›  wrote:
…[66 more quoted lines elided]…
› display numeric field on S/360).

Thank you, Robert.

The above gels with what I remember.

After nearly 50 years, memory can become, as the Doc says: "porous".

To me now, it seems like a different dream, long ago and far away. But the
lessons learned and the excitement of learning them is still the same... :-)

I spent most of yesterday learning how to use C# reflection in order to
write a general purpose "COBOL record handler".

(I did this because I don't want to have to reference every field in a
record specifically when trying to build SQL statements. Reflecting a given
record in LINQ means that I can have a single loop that will run through the
fields for me and it will work for ANY record... I don't have to know what
fields are in the record because the reflection gets the name and type data
of every field that is present. There is an object mapping (generated by a
tool) that allows records to be treated as objects and once they are
objects, manipulating them is really easy.)

Last night and this morning I hastily assembled some sample code to try out
what I learned.

I tried using the new code to insert, update, and delete records on a DB
using LINQ. It all worked effortlessly. I was blown away and had the same
excitement I experienced the first time I ran COBOL on a mainframe, back in
1967... :-)

Hardware and software may change, but I still do it for the same reasons I
have always done it; it makes me smile when I solve it... :-)

Pete.
"I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."
```

› nos··m@gm··l.com wrote:
› On Saturday, February 8, 2014 10:01:55 AM UTC+11, Pete Dashwood wrote:

Did I say it wasn't?
Of course it's storage-to-storage. That's what ZAP means (Zero and Add Packed).

› as you think. (Under certain circumstances...)

slower than AP. If ZAP is slow, so are AP and SP. But they aren't.

- **References:** `<0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209bcc8174aa@googlegroups.com> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p8@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p9@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p10@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p21@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p22@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p23@usenetarchives.gap>`

```
On Mon, 10 Feb 2014 07:22:30 -0800 (PST), rob··s@gm··l.com
wrote:

› On Tuesday, February 11, 2014 12:00:17 AM UTC+11, Pete Dashwood wrote:
›› nos··m@gm··l.com wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
› slower than AP.  If ZAP is slow, so are AP and SP.  But they aren't.

The issue would be the percentage of superfluous ZAPs (for negative
zero normalization) in the total number of instructions generated by
the arithmetic statements. If it is significant then in certain
circumstances it could impact run time. As someone who has done
application tuning, I only ran across one instance where I made a
significant difference in run time and this was a compute bound
program. I will assume that the original poster has got one or more
of these. I also have recently read on bit.listserv.ibm-main that
with the modern pipelined architectures run compute times can be far
more variable, i.e. running the same program with the same inputs can
give far wider variances in compute time than was previously the case.
If I had access to a z series, I would be tempted to write two
assembler programs differing only by insertion of the ZAP sign
corrections and run some tests to see the differences. Indeed this
could be a worthwhile exercise for anyone still active. This could
also help decide whether recoding to minimize these ZAPs would be
worth the effort in those cases where a program is close to being
compute bound.

Clark Morris
```

- **References:** `<0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209bcc8174aa@googlegroups.com> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p8@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p9@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p10@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p21@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p22@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p23@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p24@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p25@usenetarchives.gap>`

```
On Monday, February 10, 2014 6:41:32 PM UTC-5, rob··.@gm··l.com wrote: > On Tuesday, February 11, 2014 7:50:16 AM UTC+11, Clark F Morris wrote: > The issue would be the percentage of superfluous ZAPs (for negative zero > normalization) in the total number of instructions generated by the > arithmetic statements. You have nmisread the situation. The issue is the negative zero following an overflow. On account of that, the issue is connected with how the system handles overflow.

Correct! Wow, I didn't realize that my original post had generated such a lively conversation, mainly "OP is an idiot". I thought I'd get notified if there were

IBM admits that the ZAP 'in place' is to correct only one situation...

"There's only one case where the ZAP would make a difference. If the AP causes overflow, and if the result of the AP is zero, then the zero could have a negative sign.

The COBOL documentation says the sign for a zero should be positive (either C or F). The ZAP would change the negative sign to a positive sign. Apart from that one case, the ZAP would leave the result of the AP unchanged."

Now here's a generated code snip from a routine that is chewing up 10%+ of the CPU for our biggest daily job:

001528 FA55 704D 404D AP 77(6,7),77(6,4)
00152E F855 704D 704D ZAP 77(6,7),77(6,7)
003778 ADD
001534 5A80 9140 A 8,320(0,9)
001538 5A60 9140 A 6,320(0,9)
00153C FA55 8003 6003 AP 3(6,8),3(6,6)
001542 F855 8003 8003 ZAP 3(6,8),3(6,8)
003782 ADD
001548 FA55 7053 4053 AP 83(6,7),83(6,4)
00154E F855 7053 7053 ZAP 83(6,7),83(6,7)
003787 IF
001554 95E2 3042 CLI 66(3),X'E2'
001558 4780 B074 BC 8,116(0,11)
003790 ADD
00155C FA55 704D 4059 AP 77(6,7),89(6,4)
001562 F855 704D 704D ZAP 77(6,7),77(6,7)
003794 ADD
001568 FA55 8003 4059 AP 3(6,8),89(6,4)
00156E F855 8003 8003 ZAP 3(6,8),3(6,8)
001574 GN=85 EQU *
003799 ADD
001574 FA55 8009 4059 AP 9(6,8),89(6,4)
00157A F855 8009 8009 ZAP 9(6,8),9(6,8)
003803 ADD
001580 FA55 8009 6009 AP 9(6,8),9(6,6)
001586 F855 8009 8009 ZAP 9(6,8),9(6,8)

The fields involved in these calculations are PIC S9(09)V99 COMP-3. Therefore the only situation that these ZAP instructions are attempting to correct is if the result of the calculation is -1 billion dollars exactly. And in this case we THREW AWAY (overflowed) the BILLION DOLLARS without a whimper.

COBOL users should be able to determine that this will never happen and optionally eliminate this instruction generation for this program compile.

This would be a compile OPTION! Not a unilateral decision to change the generated code and hope that nobody's business logic was altered.
```

I think that the following code is also generated for both
NUMPROC(NOPFD) - no preferred sign, and NUMPROC(MIG) where negative
zero is considered to be numeric (Unlike NUMPROC(PFD) - preferred
sign) where it is not. Since a CP- Compare packed will treat positive
and negative zero as equal, the ZAP for NOPFD and MIG values of
NUMPROC is superfluous.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Extra ZAPs generated by COBOL after packed arithmetic function

_(reply depth: 5)_

- **From:** "cfmpublic" <ua-author-127808@usenetarchives.gap>
- **Date:** 2014-02-09T16:25:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gap-4e61c2a6d9-p35@usenetarchives.gap>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<gap-4e61c2a6d9-p34@usenetarchives.gap>`
- **References:** `<0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209bcc8174aa@googlegroups.com> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p8@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p32@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p33@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p34@usenetarchives.gap>`

```
On Sun, 9 Feb 2014 19:35:25 +0000 (UTC), Doug Miller
wrote:

› "Pete Dashwood"  wrote in news:bllvk7FfbsnU1
› @mid.individual.net:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
› has any evidence at all.

As a retired MVS systems programmer and COBOL applications programmer
who follows bit.listserv.ibm-main and is register to the listserve
IBM-MA··N@bam··a.edu, I am fairly certain those zaps have the
potential for slowing things. They would involve storage references
which can be problematic on pipelined computers (Zero Add Packed is a
storage to storage operation). While it is almost certain the data is
in cache give that what the OP is referring to is a Zero Add Packed in
place of the previous result in a compute or arithmetic statement, I
am not certain of the effects given that the instruction can not
execute until the previous one completes. I have written loops that
had a fair number of arithmetic computations in them and they can be
time consuming. Since the results can be different when rounding
and/or division are involved between decimal and binary arithmetic,
converting to decimal probably is not the answer. Possibly re-writing
the code to use compute statements that consolidate the arithmetic
operations would help.

A large number of instructions with immediate operands like Add
Halfword Immediate have been added to the z series instruction set to
avoid storage references. The Enterprise COBOL for z/OS V5.1 compiler
has been rewritten to use a new code base and has extensive changes -
http://www-01.ibm.com/common/ssi/cgi-bin/ssialias?subtype=ca&infotype=an&appname=iSource&supplier=897&letternum=ENUS213-144.
The options guide is
http://pic.dhe.ibm.com/infocenter/ratdevz/v8r5/topic/com.ibm.ent.cbl.zos.doc/custom/igy5cg10.pdf.

For those of you who are using the IBM product Visual Gen formerly
Cross System Product, Enterprise COBOL's elimination of NUMPROC(MIG)
may cause problems because this product forced F zones for positive
numbers. Check current manuals because this dates from 14 plus years
ago.

Clark Morris
```

- **References:** `<0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209bcc8174aa@googlegroups.com> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p8@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p32@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p33@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p34@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p35@usenetarchives.gap>`

```
On Monday, February 10, 2014 8:25:08 AM UTC+11, Clark F Morris wrote:
› As a retired MVS systems programmer and COBOL applications programmer who follows bit.listserv.ibm-main and is register to the listserve IBM-..··.@bam··a.edu, I am fairly certain those zaps have the potential for slowing things.
 
› Really?  ZAP is one of the fastest decimal instructions, along with AP.
 
› They would involve storage references which can be problematic on pipelined computers (Zero Add Packed is a storage to storage operation). While it is almost certain the data is in cache give that what the OP is referring to is a Zero Add Packed in place of the previous result in a compute or arithmetic statement, I am not certain of the effects given that the instruction can not execute until the previous one completes.
 
› As is the case with most of the decimal instructions.
 
› I have written loops that had a fair number of arithmetic computations in them and they can be time consuming. Since the results can be different when rounding and/or division are involved between decimal and binary arithmetic, converting to decimal probably is not the answer.

I expect that the OP is using decimal arithmetic. A decimal result
overflowed, and the OP's ZAP instruction is there to correct the result.
Why would he be converting his decimal arithmetic to decimal?

› Possibly re-writing the code to use compute statements that consolidate
› the arithmetic operations would help.
 
› A large number of instructions with immediate operands like Add Halfword
› Immediate have been added to the z series instruction set to avoid storage
› references.

No, they were added to reduce the need to rely on base-addressing
for constants. The S/390 etc moved to instruction-relative addressing.

Such instructions (e.g., add immediate) had been around in microprcessors
for some considerable time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Extra ZAPs generated by COBOL after packed arithmetic function

_(reply depth: 7)_

- **From:** "cfmpublic" <ua-author-127808@usenetarchives.gap>
- **Date:** 2014-02-10T15:36:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gap-4e61c2a6d9-p38@usenetarchives.gap>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<gap-4e61c2a6d9-p37@usenetarchives.gap>`
- **References:** `<0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209bcc8174aa@googlegroups.com> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p8@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p32@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p33@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p34@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p35@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p37@usenetarchives.gap>`

```
On Sun, 9 Feb 2014 19:45:22 -0800 (PST), rob··s@gm··l.com wrote:

› On Monday, February 10, 2014 8:25:08 AM UTC+11, Clark F Morris wrote:
›› As a retired MVS systems programmer and COBOL applications programmer who follows bit.listserv.ibm-main and is register to the listserve IBM-..··.@bam··a.edu, I am fairly certain those zaps have the potential for slowing things.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
› Why would he be converting his decimal arithmetic to decimal?

There are two reasons for a final ZAP being generated for an
arithmetic verb. The first is to move the result from a work area.
The second is to normalize a sign in case a negative zero is
generated. I am assuming that it is the second class of final ZAP
that is being discussed here. As Arnold Trembley pointed out, the
reason for this ZAP being generated for NUMPROC(PFD) (and I think
NUMPROC(MIG) is probably to allow CLC (Compare Logical Character)
instructions to be generated for equality compare with zero.
› 
›› Possibly re-writing the code to use compute statements that consolidate
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
› for constants.  The S/390 etc moved to instruction-relative addressing.

That would explain the Branch Relative instructions but not a number
of the other instructions.
›
› Such instructions (e.g., add immediate) had been around in microprcessors
› for some considerable time.

Agreed and for the same reason, pipelining with the need to avoid
storage references.

Clark Morris
```

On Tuesday, February 11, 2014 7:36:54 AM UTC+11, Clark F Morris wrote:
› On Sun, 9 Feb 2014 19:45:22 -0800 (PST), nos··m@gm··l.com wrote: >On Monday, February 10, 2014 8:25:08 AM UTC+11, Clark F Morris wrote: >> As a retired MVS systems programmer and COBOL applications programmer who follows bit.listserv.ibm-main and is register to the listserve IBM-..··.@bam··a.edu, I am fairly certain those zaps have the potential for slowing things. > >Really? ZAP is one of the fastest decimal instructions, along with AP. > >> They would involve storage references which can be problematic on pipelined computers (Zero Add Packed is a storage to storage operation). While it is almost certain the data is in cache give that what the OP is referring to is a Zero Add Packed in place of the previous result in a compute or arithmetic statement, I am not certain of the effects given that the instruction can not execute until the previous one completes. > >As is the case with most of the decimal instructions. > >> I have written loops that had a fair number of arithmetic computations in them and they can be time consuming. Since the results can be different when rounding and/or division are involved between decimal and binary arithmetic, converting to decimal probably is not the answer. > >I expect that the OP is using decimal arithmetic. A decimal result >overflowed, and the OP's ZAP instruction is there to correct the result. >Why would he be converting his decimal arithmetic to decimal? There are two reasons for a final ZAP being generated for an arithmetic verb. The first is to move the result from a work area. The second is to normalize a sign in case a negative zero is generated. I am assuming that it is the second class of final ZAP that is being discussed here. As Arnold Trembley pointed out, the reason for this ZAP being generated for NUMPROC(PFD) (and I think NUMPROC(MIG) is probably to allow CLC (Compare Logical Character) instructions to be generated for equality compare with zero. > >> Possibly re-writing the code to use compute statements that consolidate >> the arithmetic operations would help. > >> A large number of instructions with immediate operands like Add Halfword >> Immediate have been added to the z series instruction set to avoid storage >> references. > >No, they were added to reduce the need to rely on base-addressing >for constants. The S/390 etc moved to instruction-relative addressing.
 
› That would explain the Branch Relative instructions but not a number of the
› other instructions.

Branch instructions are not the main problem, as they can typically
be covered by a single base register.
It's the data area that's a problem, because one or more base registers
are required to access it.
However, addressing generally is improved by having relative addressing,
hence the move.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Extra ZAPs generated by COBOL after packed arithmetic function

- **From:** "steveuk529" <ua-author-14501714@usenetarchives.gap>
- **Date:** 2014-04-29T17:11:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gap-4e61c2a6d9-p42@usenetarchives.gap>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<gap-4e61c2a6d9-p32@usenetarchives.gap>`
- **References:** `<0ef1eeb1-e8e7-4e36-857e-209bcc8174aa@googlegroups.com> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p8@usenetarchives.gap> <gap-4e61c2a6d9-p32@usenetarchives.gap>`

```
Maybe IBM is going to have a good laugh but they have apparently accepted my argument and are looking at the possibilty of introducing a new packed field definition to accomodate my request. They felt that a compiler option would be too broad and a safer way to allow the ZAP to be removed was to specify a new comp(-3) type.

IBM comment on RFE: "The feature, for a new comp type that would have these relaxed sign code restrictions so the scope of the change could be limited to known safe cases in the code, has been prioritized, but not put into plan yet. Once it has been put into plan, this RFE will be updated."

Uncommitted Candidate: This request may not be delivered within the release currently under development, but the theme is aligned with the current multi-year strategy. IBM may consider and evaluate any RFE Community feedback for this request through activities such as voting. IBM will update this request in the future.
```
